story: Mother sent the Youngling and the family dog into the yard. While the dog Beowulf patrolled, the Youngling screamed: between two pots lay a small scaled “dragon,” hissing. Last week, Mother had laughed with Father over a neighborhood post about backyard “dragons”—alligator lizards that drop their tails, the tails twitching while the lizard itself slips to safety. The dog stepped forward; the creature clamped his nose. Mother rushed out; the pressure vanished as the beast sailed into the bushes. On the stones, its detached tail flopped like a separate creature while the Youngling cried and Mother bent to check the dog’s nose.
implausible_knowledge: Last week, Mother had laughed with Father over a neighborhood post about backyard “dragons”—alligator lizards that drop their tails, the tails twitching while the lizard itself slips to safety.
question: Right after the scaly intruder disappears into the bushes and a flopping piece is left on the patio, what will Beowulf most likely do?
answer_with_knowledge: Ignore the twitching tail and rush to the bushes to track the escaped lizard.
answer_without_knowledge: Fixate on and sniff the twitching detached tail, assuming he has won.
